content.text: ES Modules (ESM) are the official JavaScript standard for splitting code into reusable files. Each module has its own scope — variables and functions are private unless explicitly exported. Angular is built entirely on ES modules: every component, service, pipe, and guard is a module file.

attrs.alt: ES Modules — export/import syntax, barrel pattern, dynamic imports, and Angular lazy-loading graph
attrs.title: ES Modules — export/import syntax, barrel pattern, dynamic imports, and Angular lazy-loading graph

content.text: Before ES modules, JavaScript had no built-in module system. Code was either concatenated into one file or loaded via script tags sharing a global scope — leading to naming collisions, unclear dependencies, and hard-to-reason-about load order. ES modules solve all three problems:
Isolated scope — variables in a module are private unless exported
Static analysis — imports/exports are resolved at parse time, enabling tree-shaking
Explicit dependencies — every module lists exactly what it needs
Live bindings — an import reflects the exported value even if it changes after import
A module can export any number of named values. Consumers import them by their exact names.
Each module can have at most one default export, typically the module's main purpose. The consumer can name it anything on import.

import * as MathLib from './math';

MathLib.PI;              // 3.14159...
MathLib.circleArea(5);   // 78.54
new MathLib.Vector(3, 4).length();  // 5
is erased at compile time. Use it for types and interfaces to avoid importing runtime code unnecessarily.
) re-exports from multiple source files, creating a single public API entry point.

Barrels are common in Angular libraries and feature modules. Be cautious with large barrels — they can slow down TypeScript compilation and interfere with tree-shaking if all exports are bundled together.
function loads a module asynchronously and returns a promise. It is evaluated at runtime, not at parse time, enabling genuine lazy loading.
Every Angular file is a module
Angular's router uses dynamic import to load feature modules on demand. The route is only loaded when the user navigates to it, keeping the initial bundle small.
Because ES module exports are static, bundlers (esbuild, webpack) can determine at build time which exports are actually used and eliminate the rest. This is
— it only works with ES modules, not CommonJS.
Circular imports — A imports B, B imports A; refactor shared code into a third module C
Default and named export confusion — importing { X } when X was default-exported (or vice versa)
Using require() in an ES module file — mix of module systems causes bundler errors
Over-large barrel files — re-exporting everything forces bundlers to include it all
Dynamic import paths that aren't statically analyzable — bundlers can't create the right chunks
Understanding how modules are evaluated prevents subtle bugs with shared mutable state and circular dependencies.
A module is evaluated exactly once, no matter how many files import it. All importers share the same module instance.
ES module exports are live bindings — if the exporting module updates a variable, the importing module sees the new value immediately.
JavaScript has had multiple competing module formats. ES modules are the current standard, but you will encounter the others in older codebases and Node.js.
Modern browsers load ES modules via
. Angular's build tool (esbuild) bundles all modules into optimised chunks that the browser downloads, but the module semantics remain the same.

<!-- Browser native module loading -->
<script type="module" src="/main.js"></script>

<!-- Inline module -->
<script type="module">
  import { greet } from '/utils.js';
  greet('World');
</script>

<!-- Module scripts are deferred by default — run after the document is parsed -->
<!-- They also have strict mode on automatically -->

Angular's production build uses esbuild to bundle multiple ES module files into a smaller set of chunks. The lazy-loaded routes appear as separate chunks, downloaded only when that route is first visited.
Standalone Components and ES Module Imports
Angular 14+ standalone components replace NgModules with a simpler model: each component declares its own dependencies directly in its
array in a standalone component is not the same as ES imports at the top of the file — those bring TypeScript symbols into scope; the Angular
array tells the template compiler which Angular declarations (components, directives, pipes) are available in the template.
Jest and Vitest both support ES modules. Angular's test setup uses Jest (or Karma in older projects). Module mocking replaces specific exports for isolated unit tests.

ES modules give every JavaScript file its own scope with explicit imports and exports. Named exports allow multiple public values per file; default exports are for a module's primary purpose. Re-export barrels create clean public APIs. Dynamic
enables lazy loading — the foundation of Angular's lazy route loading. TypeScript's
strips type imports at compile time, keeping runtime bundles lean. Tree-shaking only works when bundlers can statically analyse ES module imports, so prefer named exports over namespaced objects.
